topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the chipped coffee table, a vibrant splash of color against the dull beige of my living room. I picked it up with a hesitant finger, the paper crinkling under my touch. Inside, a bouquet of deep purple irises and some stiff, almost unnaturally perfect roses. There was no note, just the flowers, starkly beautiful and utterly impersonal. I stared at them, my shoulders slumping further as I traced the velvety petals. A tightness began to coil in my chest, a familiar, heavy pressure that made my breath catch. I didn't move to put them in a vase; instead, I just let my gaze drift over the arrangement, feeling a profound sense of being overlooked, as if this sudden burst of attention was just another unwelcome demand.

[story 2]

When the delivery driver handed over the wrapped package, I barely registered the sound. It was just another interruption to the quiet ache that had settled deep in my bones all week. The flowers were arranged with an almost aggressive symmetry—too perfect, perhaps. I took the box, my fingers brushing against the cool stems. As I carried it to the kitchen, a wave of weariness washed over me, heavy and slow. I set the box down on the counter, avoiding eye contact with the vibrant blooms. I leaned against the counter, closing my eyes for a moment, trying to regulate the tremor in my hands, wishing the silence could swallow the unwelcome intrusion of such bright, unsolicited gesture.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked into the entryway, abandoned on the welcome mat. They were a riot of saturated hues, a stark contrast to the grey monotony of the hallway. I picked them up, the stems cool against my palm. A strange, hollow feeling expanded in my stomach, a dull ache that seemed to vibrate with the sheer effort of feeling something so intensely. I didn't know who had sent them, or why. All I could do was stand there, clutching the arrangement, my jaw tight, a silent protest rising in my throat. I turned away quickly, needing distance from the unsolicited warmth they represented, and retreated into the shadows of the apartment.
